coredata.dc:title: Micro-RNA detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer of DNA-carbon quantum dots probes
coredata.dc:creator: Khakbaz, Faeze
Mahani, Mohamad
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots have been proposed as an effective platform for miRNA detection. Carbon dots were synthesized by citric acid. The synthesized dots were characterized by dynamic light scattering, UV-Vis spectrophotometry, spectrofluorimetry, transmission electron microscopy and FT-IR spectrophotometry. The fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized dots was determined using quinine sulfate as the standard. The FAM-labeled single stranded DNA, as sensing element, was adsorbed on dots by π-π interaction. The quenching of the dots fluorescence due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was used for mir 9-1 detection. In the presence of the complementary miRNA, the FRET did not take place and the fluorescence was recovered.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Mir 9-1
FRET
Single stranded DNA
FAM
Quantum yield

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FT-IR spectra of carbon quantum dots.
The regression of fluorescence intensity on absorbance for different concentrations of quinine homo sulfate (a) and synthesized carbon quantum dots (b).
Fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots, FAM and prepared probe in the absence of target (a). Fluorescence intensity of the prepared probe in the presence of mir 9-1 (b).

head.ce:title: Micro-RNA detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer of DNA-carbon quantum dots probes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Nanochemistry, Faculty of Sciences and Modern Technologies, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Sciences and Modern Technologies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots have been proposed as an effective platform for miRNA detection. Carbon dots were synthesized by citric acid. The synthesized dots were characterized by dynamic light scattering, UV-Vis spectrophotometry, spectrofluorimetry, transmission electron microscopy and FT-IR spectrophotometry. The fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized dots was determined using quinine sulfate as the standard. The FAM-labeled single stranded DNA, as sensing element, was adsorbed on dots by π-π interaction. The quenching of the dots fluorescence due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was used for mir 9-1 detection. In the presence of the complementary miRNA, the FRET did not take place and the fluorescence was recovered.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, breast cancer rates have risen and many researches have been conducted on this type of cancer [1–6]. Screening programs have been promoted to detect breast cancer at an early stage when they are too small to cause any symptoms. At this stage, the probability of successful treatment is higher [7]. Mammography is a routine method for the diagnosis of breast cancer. This method cannot detect about 60–70% of breast cancers, so development of new methods for this purpose is a demand [8].

MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are a group of small, noncoding, endogenous, regulatory and single-stranded RNAs that play impressive roles in gene expression regulation [9–11]. Due to their role in the control of physiological and pathological processes, mi-RNAs have been used as biomarkers for several disease such as viral infections [12–15], cardiovascular disorders [16,17], and cancers [18–21]. For some reasons such as high stability of mi-RNAs in formalin-fixed tissues [22–24], misregulation and improper expression of mi-RNA in various cancers [25,26] and tissue-specific expression patterns of miRNAs in cancers [20], mi RNAs could be an ideal biosensor for cancers. To date, many techniques such as Northern blots [27–30], in situ hybridization [31–33], real-time quantitative PCR [34–36], and microarrays [37–39] have been developed for miRNA detection. But all of them have some restriction such as expensive equipment, which limit their applications. Recently some fluorescence biosensors such as silver nanoclusters [40–42] and semiconductor quantum dots [43,44] were designed for mi-RNA detection.

Recently variety of nanostructures such as metallic nanoparticles, quantum dots and carbon based nanostructures have been used widely in the development of rapid, low cost and simple biosensors based on electrochemical and fluorometric methods [45,46]. Carbon quantum dots refer to fluorescent graphite nanocrystals that are about 1–10 nm in size [47–50]. CQDs were synthesized via laser ablation of graphite powder and cement in 2006 [51]. Carbon quantum dots are attracting considerable attention due to their several favorable attribute including small size, chemical inertness, low toxicity and environmental friendly, size dependent luminescence emission and water solubility [52–56].

Several studies have shown that the carbon nanostructures can adsorb single stranded DNAs via π-π interactions, and these materials have been used as a dye quencher in dye-labeled single-stranded probes [13,57–61]. In 2008 carbon nanotubes were used for detection of the specific DNA [60], but the low solubility and the large size of CNT limit its applications in biosensors. Graphene oxide [61], carbon nanoparticles [57,58,62] and carbon nitride nanosheets [59] were successfully used as a dye quencher for miRNA detections.

In this work carbon quantum dots were synthesized by a facile, cost effective and one step method. The synthesized carbon quantum dots were characterized and used as a useful fluorescent platform for miRNA detection. The response mechanism of the probe is based on the adsorption of the FAM-labeled single-stranded DNA (ss-DNA) on the carbon quantum dots through π-π interactions, which caused to quenching of the fluorescence intensity. In the presence of miR9-1, a duplex helix was formed and the double-stranded DNA/miRNA released from the surface of CQD and the fluorescence intensity is recovered.

Citric acid, sodium chloride, potassium chloride, Na2HPO4.2H2O and K2HPO4 were purchased from Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany). 2, 2′-(Ethylenedioxy) bis (ethylamine) and quinine homo sulfate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (Saint Louis, Missouri, USA). All of nucleic acids that explained in Table S1 were purchased from Takapuzist Co. (Tehran, Iran).

The size distribution and zeta potential analysis of carbon quantum dots were measured by a Zeta sizer NanoZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, Worcestershire, UK) at 25 °C. The morphology and size of synthesized carbon quantum dots characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi, Japan) the surface functional properties were investigated by FT-IR spectrophotometer (Bruker TENSOR 27, Germany), and The optical properties of as-prepared carbon quantum dot studied by carry50 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia) and carry Eclipse spectrofluorimeter (Varian, Australia).

Carbon quantum dots were synthesized by a facile and cost effective heating method [63,64] 2 g citric acid were dissolved in 10 ml distilled water and stirred. Under vigorous stirring 800 μl 2, 2’-(Ethylenedioxy) bis (ethylamine) were added. The solution was heated at 270 °C. Until the color changes to dark brown. When the brown crystal (carbon quantum dots) were synthesized, the solution was cooled to room temperature and 20 ml deionized water were added. The solution was centrifuged at 21000g for 30 min. The temperature, time and amount of carbon source as the factors affecting the synthesis procedure were optimized by Taguchi method.

The quantum yield of CQDs was calculated by comparative method [65–67]. To calculate the quantum yield of prepared carbon dots, quinine sulfate solution was chosen as reference. Electronic absorption and fluorescent spectra of quantum dots and reference were recorded in five different concentrations and the data were used for quantum yield calculations.

MTT assays were used to determine the cell activity, in brief MCF-7 cells were cultured onto a 96-well micro plate with Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin for 24 h in 5% CO2 at 37 °c. Different volumes of synthesized carbon quantum dots solution were loaded to each well, as each concentration has three duplications and incubated for 24 h. After that 20 μl of 5 mg/ml 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution was added to each cell well, and incubated for 4 h. The culture medium was removed and 150 μl DMSO was added to release the formazan. The mixture was shaken about for 15 min and optical density was studied by microplate reader. The cell viability was calculated by the following formula.
Cell
Viability
( % )
=
(
OD
Treated
/
OD
Control
)
× 100 %

To form the functionalized carbon quantum dot probes 100 μl of 1 μM FAM-single stranded DNA was dispersed in 1660 μl 0.1 M phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (pH = 7.4) and stirred for about 15 min to achieved a homogenous solution. The fluorescence spectra of the dye were recorded. Different amounts of carbon quantum dot solution (40 μg/L) were added and dispersed at room temperature, and fluorescence spectra of the carbon quantum dot and dye were recorded. The probes were stored at 4 °C.
Different amounts of mir 9-1 were added to the probes solution and the fluorescence spectra of CQDs and FAM were recorded.

Carbon quantum dots were prepared by a facile and cost effective procedure. CQDs brown clusters were formed from heating of the citric acid and 2, 2′-(Ethylenedioxy) bis (ethylamine) solution. The factors affecting the synthesis procedure were studied in two and four levels including temperature (150 and 270 °C), amounts of carbon source (2 and 4 g) and dissolution time (2, 24, 72 h and a week). The fluorescence intensity was selected as the response variable. Taguchi method was used for experimental design. The responses were analyzed by Taguchi method and 2 g citric acid, 270 °C and one-week dissolution time were selected as the optimum conditions.

Fig. S1 shows the electronic absorption spectra of carbon quantum dots in the UV-Vis region. The broad band absorption in UV region due to π-π∗ (CC) and n-π∗ (CO) transitions are similar to literature's spectra [50–59]. Fig. S2 shows the excitation- emission fluorescence spectra and Fig. S3 shows the emission spectra of CQDs in different excitation wavelength.

As it can be observed in Fig. S2, the prepared CQDs show significant blue fluorescence emissions by excitation at 330 nm. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs exhibits the maximum excitation at 330 nm and the maximum emission at 460 nm. In literature different reasons including surface trap sites and polydispersity index were mentioned for the excitation dependent spectrum of CQDs [53]. But in this work as some reports [47] synthesized CQDs show excitation independent emission when excited from 250 nm to 400 nm (Fig. S3).

The average size of carbon quantum dots was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS). DLS was performed at 633 nm and a scattering angle of 90°. In this technique, the diffusion of particles causing by Brownian motion is measured and converts to the size and size distribution by the Stokes Einstein relationship. As shown in Fig. S4 the particle size distribution of synthesized CQDs is about 1–3 nm with an average size of 1.5 nm. As it can be observed from Fig. S5, the average size of the CQDs do not change significantly even after 6 months. Thus there is no need for fresh synthesis.

The morphology and the size distribution of carbon quantum dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). For TEM imaging, the solution was placed on a copper grid end evaporated. TEM image (Fig. S6) reveals that the as-prepared CQDs are monodisperse nanocrystals of near spherical morphology with an average diameter of 3 nm.

Carbon quantum dots are generally composed of three main elements carbon, oxygen and hydrogen. In several works carbon dots were modified to increase fluorescence quantum yield and decrees the cytotoxicity. In this work citric acid were used as carbon Source and 2, 2′-(Ethylenedioxy) bis (ethylamine) as the co-reactant and amine source. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was used for identification of the surface functional groups. The presence of COOH, COH and NH functional groups on CQDs can be observed from Fig. 1 these groups cause the high water solubility and stability of synthesized carbon quantum dots [38,68].

The most reliable method for calculating the fluorescence quantum yield is the comparative method. Quantum yield of carbon quantum dots were calculated by the following equation.
Q x
=
Q r
(
m x
/
m r
)
(
η x 2
/
η r 2
)
Where Qx and Qr refers to quantum yield of carbon dots and reference respectively. mx and mr are slopes of the regression of fluorescence intensities on absorbances for five different concentrations of CQD and quinine sulfate respectively. η2 x and η2 r are the refractive index of carbon quantum dots and quinine sulfate solution respectively.

The fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots and quinine sulfate (QY = 55%) was recorded at five different absorbances (i.e. five different concentrations). Two regression equations were obtained for quinine sulfate (Fig. 2 a) and CQDs (Fig. 2b). The calculated quantum yield of the carbon dots at excitation wavelength was about 8%.

In several studies, the toxicity of synthesized carbon quantum dots were studied by MTT assays [64,66,69–73]. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of synthesized carbon quantum dots, MCF-7 cells were treated with various concentration of CQDs from 5 to 120 μg/L for 24 h. Fig. S7 shows that the treated cells exhibited viability from 99.53% to 94.31%.

A probe has been designed based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) assay to monitor mir9-1 as a marker for breast cancer. The FAM labeled single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) was adsorbed on CQDs by π-π interaction. As shown in Fig. S8 the presence of amine groups on the CQDs surface led to a positive zeta potential of +5 mV. ssDNA adsorbed on the CQDs, shows the zeta potential shift to negative values due to the negatively-charged phosphate of the ssDNA. Due to the adsorption of FAM-ssDNA on the CQDs, the energy is transferred from CQDs as a donor to the FAM as an acceptor in a non-radiative resonance mode (FRET). In the absence of resonance energy transfer, the emission of CQDs and FAM can be observed in 450 and 520 nm by excitation at 330 and 490 nm respectively (Fig. 3 a).

Fig. 3a shows the resonance energy transfer between CQDs and FAM-ssDNA in the absence of mir 9-1. The efficiency of the energy transfer can be calculated from the relative fluorescence intensities of the CQDs in the presence (IDA) and absence (ID) of acceptor (FAM).
E = 1 −
(
I
DA
/
I D
)

The efficiency of energy transfer for a single donor–acceptor pair at a fixed distance can be calculated by:
E =
R 6
/
R 6
+
r 6
Where the R is the Forster distance. In this case, the efficiency of the energy transfer is about 74% and the distance between donor and acceptor is about 0.84 R. So the FAM – CQD distance is in the order of Forster distance due to π-π interaction between CQDs and ss-DNA.

The formation of a FAM- labeled DNA – miRNA duplex helix was theoretically feasible due to the maximum ΔG of −37.54 kcal/mol. In the presence of mir 9-1, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs is recovered as its value in the absence of FAM-ssDNA (Fig. 3b). This is due to duplex helix formation between mir 9-1 and ssDNA that results in elimination of π-π interaction. The interactions between the formed duplex helix strand and CQD are so weak that the duplex helix strand is away from the surface of CQD, and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer cannot take place.

The selectivity of probe was evaluated by using a non-complementary oligonucleotide and three base mismatch, as shown in Fig. S9 no fluorescence recovery is observed in the presence of these oligonucleotides.

A probe was developed for detection of mir 9-1 based on carbon quantum dots (CQD). FAM-labeled single stranded DNA was adsorbed on high fluorescence quantum yield, water dispersible and low cytotoxicity CQDs. The CQDs fluorescence quenching due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer between CQDs and FAM was used as physical property for detection. The probe can be used for detection of mir 9-1 in urine for early detection of breast cancer.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ab.2017.01.025.
